name: Pinkerton-informed raw live-band rock drums: Sound City / Electric Lady reconstruction

candidateSetupLabels.name: Pinkerton-Informed Minimum Rock Room
candidateSetupLabels.note: Label and channel grouping only; no planner coordinates created in this pass.

candidateSetupLabels.name: Pinkerton-Informed Sound City Expanded
candidateSetupLabels.note: Label and channel grouping only; no compact app template entry created in this pass.

accuracyBoundary.summary: No published Pinkerton-specific drum mic sheet was verified. This entry combines confirmed session context with a practical 1990s live-rock drum reconstruction.
doUseAs: Sound City / Electric Lady-informed planning reference
Practical raw live-band rock drum setup
doNotUseAs: Proof that Pinkerton used the Nevermind drum setup

sources.title: Pinkerton (album)
sources.reliability: reasonably strong for broad album, studio, production, and personnel context; verify against physical or official credits before treating as session paperwork
supports: Electric Lady and Sound City session blocks

sources.title: Sound City Studios
sources.reliability: useful context for studio identity and rock discography; not Pinkerton-specific drum mic documentation
supports: Sound City reputation and historical rock context
Nirvana and other rock artist context

sources.title: How Dave Grohl delivered the Smells Like Teen Spirit drum track
sources.reliability: medium as analogy only; does not document Pinkerton
supports: Nevermind Sound City mic reference: D12 kick, SM57 snare, possible AKG 451 under snare, 421 toms, 414 overheads, U87 rooms 15-18 ft back

soundGoal.summary: Raw, live, aggressive, medium-room alternative-rock drums with close-mic punch, audible room interaction, controlled cymbals, and roughness left intact.

roomContext.practicalSubstitute: Use a medium-to-large live room with hard floor reflections, controlled but lively ambience, and enough space for room mics 10-18 ft from the kit. Avoid a small dead booth unless adding hallway, chamber, or re-amped room depth.

presets.label: Electric Lady Studio A live room
presets.source: Electric Lady publishes Studio A live room as 35 ft x 38 ft; height was not found in the checked source.

presets.label: Sound City live-room approximation
presets.source: Exact Sound City dimensions were not verified; planner size is based on documented Sound City room/studio context plus the 15-18 ft room-mic working distance in the Sound City comparison source.

confirmed: Patrick Wilson played drums on the album.
Karl Koch is credited with percussion on Butterfly.

snare: 14x6.5 metal snare for crack and body
14x5.5 or 14x6.5 maple snare as woodier alternate
minimal damping with some ring intact
cymbals: ride that does not wash out vocal range

micList.pattern: cardioid or hypercardioid depending on mic
micList.placement: Ported head: just inside or at the hole, aimed between beater impact and shell center. Intact front head: 4-10 inches off resonant head.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Approximately beater height or slightly below center; 0-30 degrees off-axis to reduce click.
micList.phaseRelationship: Use kick close as low-frequency anchor. Check polarity against overheads and room mics. Preserve some room delay unless low end collapses.
micList.processingNotes: Moderate EQ; weight around 60-80 Hz, reduce 250-500 Hz boxiness if needed, add 3-5 kHz only if buried by guitars. Compress 2-5 dB with dbx 160 / 1176 / Distressor-style behavior. Avoid modern metal click.
micList.evidenceBasis: AKG D12 kick is documented for Nevermind at Sound City, not Pinkerton.

micList.source: Kick drum outside / front-of-kit low support
micList.pattern: cardioid, or omni if the room is excellent
micList.placement: 1-3 ft in front of resonant head, centered or slightly off-axis.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Roughly kick-center height; angle off-axis until low end is full without excessive beater slap.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check polarity against kick-in and overheads. Move the mic physically before digital delay.
micList.processingNotes: Low-pass or darken if cymbal bleed is harsh. Blend under kick-in for body. Compress lightly or not at all.
micList.evidenceBasis: Practical modern addition; not documented for Pinkerton.

micList.placement: 1-2 inches above rim, 1-2 inches inside hoop, aimed toward center.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 30-45 degrees downward; rotate to minimize hi-hat bleed.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against overheads first, then kick. If snare bottom is used, flip bottom polarity as a starting point and choose by ear.
micList.processingNotes: High-pass around 80-120 Hz. Add 180-250 Hz body if thin; add 5-8 kHz crack if needed. Use fast 1176-style compression only enough to stabilize the backbeat. Do not remove all ring.
micList.evidenceBasis: SM57 snare is documented for Nevermind at Sound City, not Pinkerton.

micList.placement: 1-3 inches under the snare, aimed toward wires but not directly at the loudest rattle point.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Close under-shell placement; angle to reduce kick and hi-hat spill.
micList.phaseRelationship: Usually polarity-inverted relative to snare top, then verified in mono.
micList.processingNotes: High-pass aggressively. Blend only enough to add wire definition. Distortion or compression can work if blended low.
micList.evidenceBasis: AKG 451 under-snare is only supported by the Nevermind analogy.

micList.placement: 1-3 inches above head, near rim, aimed toward center.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 30-45 degrees down; angle null toward nearest crash.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check tom close mics against overheads. Avoid heavy polarity changes that thin the snare image.
micList.processingNotes: Minimal gating. Use manual cleanup if needed. Add low-mid body, cut cardboard, compress only if fills disappear.
micList.evidenceBasis: MD421 toms are documented for Nevermind at Sound City, not Pinkerton.

micList.placement: 1-4 inches above head, aimed halfway between rim and center.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 30-45 degrees; avoid pointing directly at cymbal edge.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against overheads and rack toms. Floor tom polarity can alter the whole kit low-mid image.
micList.processingNotes: Keep weight but control boom. Gate only if sympathetic resonance creates arrangement problems.
micList.evidenceBasis: Practical 1990s rock drum reconstruction; no Pinkerton-specific mic data found.

micOptions: Coles/Royer ribbons for darker cymbal picture
micList.pattern: cardioid or wide cardioid; omni only in a strong room
micList.placement: Spaced pair or wide quasi-ORTF above kit. Exact Pinkerton overhead technique is unknown.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Start 3-5 ft above cymbals, both mics equidistant from snare. Raise if cymbals are aggressive; lower if drums lack immediacy.
micList.phaseRelationship: Measure to snare. Check mono before tracking. Adjust until snare remains centered and full.
micList.processingNotes: High-pass between 80-150 Hz depending on close-mic low end. Avoid excessive top-end boost. Prefer tape/console saturation over bright modern EQ.
micList.evidenceBasis: AKG 414 overheads are documented for Nevermind at Sound City, not Pinkerton.

micList.pattern: cardioid, omni, or figure-8 depending on room
micList.placement: Start 10-18 ft in front of kit, facing snare/kick center. Move until room gives punch rather than wash.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Chest to head height first. Try lower knee-height placement if room sounds too bright.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check polarity against close-mic kit. Preserve natural delay for size. If room image smears backbeat, move mics before alignment.
micList.processingNotes: Compress hard in parallel with 1176 all-buttons, Distressor, SSL listen-mic style, dbx 160 pair, or equivalent. High-pass enough to keep kick close solid. Low-pass abrasive cymbals. Blend under close mics.
micList.evidenceBasis: Strongly supported as a Sound City-informed inference; U87 rooms 15-18 ft back are documented for Nevermind, not Pinkerton.

micList.placement: 3-6 ft in front of kit, aimed at kick/snare intersection.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Knee to waist height. Lower gives kick/tom density; higher gives snare/cymbal bite.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against kick, snare, and overheads. Use polarity for maximum punch, not maximum brightness.
micList.processingNotes: Heavy compression and saturation. Blend as aggression, not fidelity. Mute or automate down if it clouds verses.
micList.evidenceBasis: Practical substitute for raw room energy if room is small; not documented for Pinkerton.

micList.placement: 4-8 inches above outer edge, aimed away from snare.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Angle across cymbal rather than straight down to reduce harsh air blasts.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against overheads; often leave muted unless articulation is needed.
micList.processingNotes: High-pass aggressively. Use sparingly.
micList.evidenceBasis: No Pinkerton hi-hat mic documentation found.

variations.name: Electric Lady variation
variations.notes: Use the same close/overhead framework, but assume less dependence on Sound City's particular room unless stronger sources prove otherwise. Blend room mics for aggression rather than huge depth.

variations.name: Sound City variation
variations.notes: Let room mics matter more, because the Sound City room is part of the practical target.

variations.name: Butterfly variation
variations.notes: Treat as a separate acoustic/percussion configuration rather than the main full-kit rock setup.
variations.confidence: confirmed percussion credit / inferred setup separation

albumRationale: Do not chase exact undocumented gear as if it were proven.
Choose a room with a real drum identity.
Track a strong live drummer without over-sanitizing the kit.
Keep cymbals controlled at the source.
Use close mics for punch and room/crush mics for violence and depth.
Avoid modern over-editing unless the arrangement demands it.

evidenceTable.practicalTakeaway: Use 1990s alternative-rock production assumptions, not modern pop-punk assumptions.
Multiple sessions; do not assume one unified drum setup.
Performance feel matters more than replacement samples.

evidenceTable.confirmedInformation: Jack Joseph Puig credited with mixing and engineering.
evidenceTable.practicalTakeaway: Mix should retain rawness but still have professional density.

evidenceTable.confirmedInformation: Joe Barresi, Dave Fridmann, Adam Kasper, Clif Norrell, Rob Jacobs, Jim Rondinelli.
evidenceTable.practicalTakeaway: Multiple engineers make one exact universal setup unlikely.

evidenceTable.confirmedInformation: Why Bother?, Getchoo, No Other One, and Tired of Sex tracked at Electric Lady.
evidenceTable.practicalTakeaway: Do not attribute all drum sounds to Sound City.

evidenceTable.confirmedInformation: El Scorcho, Pink Triangle, The Good Life, Across the Sea, and Falling for You are tied to Sound City sessions.
evidenceTable.practicalTakeaway: Book a room with drum character for the closest practical translation.

evidenceTable.confirmedInformation: Karl Koch described Sound City as significant to the sound.
evidenceTable.practicalTakeaway: Room choice is a primary decision.

evidenceTable.confirmedInformation: Not publicly documented in sources checked.
evidenceTable.practicalTakeaway: Build a flexible equivalent setup, not a fake historical replica.

evidenceTable.confirmedInformation: Nevermind setup discussed as D12 kick, SM57 snare, 421 toms, 414 overheads, U87 rooms 15-18 ft back.
evidenceTable.practicalTakeaway: Use as a starting template for Sound City-style rock drums, not as Pinkerton proof.

evidenceTable.confirmedInformation: Not publicly documented for Pinkerton in sources checked.
evidenceTable.practicalTakeaway: Start natural; add subtle reinforcement only if the mix fails.

evidenceTable.confirmedInformation: Not publicly documented in sources checked.
evidenceTable.practicalTakeaway: Choose by sonic target: hard-hitting 1990s rock kit, controlled cymbals.

evidenceTable.confirmedInformation: Karl Koch credited with percussion on Butterfly.
evidenceTable.practicalTakeaway: Treat separately from the main kit setup.

engineeringChecklist: Move mics before using digital alignment.
Let the drummer balance the kit physically.
Commit to room tone if the room sounds good.
studioBookingQuestions: Can the room support room mics 10-18 ft from the kit?
What console and preamps are available?
Can the engineer commit to phase checking before EQ?
Is tape or tape-style saturation available?
Can the session prioritize drum tuning and room placement before close-mic tweaking?
Can the studio provide a hallway, chamber, or alternate ambient space if the live room is too small?
unresolvedQuestions: Exact snare, cymbals, heads, sticks, tuning, and muffling.
Exact kick, snare, tom, overhead, room, and spot microphones.
Exact mic placements and phase method.
Exact preamps, console channels, outboard, tape speed, tape formulation, and noise reduction.
Whether samples or triggers were used.
Exact baffle, gobo, riser, and room-mic balance choices.
Whether a physical booklet or official deluxe-edition source documents more details.

name: Interpol / Antics / Tarquin room-heavy hybrid drum configuration

candidateSetupLabels.name: Antics Minimum Room Hybrid
candidateSetupLabels.note: Label and channel grouping only; no planner coordinates created in this pass.

candidateSetupLabels.name: Antics Tarquin Expanded Room
candidateSetupLabels.note: Label and channel grouping only; no compact app template entry created in this pass.

accuracyBoundary.summary: This entry uses an Antics-era Tape Op interview as the primary source for room-heavy drums, two snare choices, selective click use, Sound Replacer restraint, and hybrid tape/Pro Tools/Neotek workflow. Close-mic specifics remain undocumented.
doUseAs: Practical template source for close mics plus serious room mics
doNotUseAs: Proof of song-by-song snare or room-mic choices

sources.title: Interpol: Attic recording
sources.reliability: high for Antics-specific drum room use, snares, kit/head preparation, click use, Sound Replacer use, tape/Pro Tools workflow, and Neotek stem mixing
supports: amp isolation leaving main room open for drums
fresh heads except broken-in snare head
6.5 inch brass snare and 5 inch wood snare
Altec 639 and Telefunken ELA M 251 extreme-room options
Pro Tools to Neotek console and back

sources.title: Tarquin Studios: Making records in the attic
sources.reliability: high for Tarquin room/build/gear context; medium for Antics-specific drum reconstruction because it predates Antics
supports: 1176, LA4, Tube-Tech CL1B, Neve 33609, Distressor, dbx 162 context

sources.title: I use amp modellers all the time... mostly not on guitar
sources.reliability: medium; useful for later confirmation of Katis/Tarquin working context, not detailed Antics drum-session documentation

sources.title: Antics (album)
sources.reliability: medium; useful for broad credits/date/studio context but not a primary scan of the booklet

soundGoal.summary: Controlled, warm, direct post-punk/indie-rock drums with close-mic punch and meaningful room movement. The room should add breath and depth without turning the kit into arena-rock wash.
confirmedDirection: room mics were a major part of the drum tone
Sound Replacer was used only lightly
performance movement was preserved where useful
avoid: full-song rigid grid unless arrangement needs it
time-aligning all room delay out of the performance

confirmed: Antics was recorded with Peter Katis at Tarquin Studios in Bridgeport, Connecticut.
The band tracked in a rehearsal-style arrangement so Fogarino could see the other members.
Paul Banks's guitar amp was in an iso booth.
Daniel Kessler's guitar amp was in a reflective storage-room/closet area with hardwood floors.
Carlos Dengler's bass amp was in a smaller closet.
The main room was left open for drums.
Fogarino described the room as medium-sized rather than gigantic.
studioBuildContext: The Tarquin profile describes the studio as moved to the top of a house.
The live-room context includes wood floor.
unknown: wall and ceiling materials during Antics
whether baffles, blankets, risers, or special treatments were used
roomContext.practicalSubstitute: Use a medium live room with reflective surfaces, wood floor if possible, enough ceiling height for the kit to breathe, and a hallway/stairwell/doorway/distant-corner position for a mono extreme room mic.

presets.label: Tarquin attic/live-room approximation
presets.source: Tape Op documents Tarquin as a top-floor residential studio with a wood-floored live-room context and enough live-band isolation space; exact live-room dimensions were not found.

presets.label: Tarquin attic/live-room approximation
presets.source: Same Tarquin room approximation, with additional far-room positions for the expanded app layout. Gobo use is not confirmed.

confirmed: Fogarino brought his own kit.
He knew what heads he wanted.
He used new heads except for the snare head, which was slightly broken in.
He used a 6.5 inch brass snare and a 5 inch wood snare.

snare: 6.5 inch brass snare or deeper metal equivalent for bigger songs
5 inch wood snare or tighter/drier equivalent for contained songs
cymbals: keep hats controlled so room mics do not become cymbal-heavy

kitContext.confidence: confirmed core kit/snare/head facts / inferred substitutions

confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.practicalTakeaway: Use a dark, characterful mono room mic if an Altec is unavailable.
Use a tube LDC or smooth condenser as an alternate far-room color.

confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.confirmedInformation: Room mics were used substantially and added breath around the close sound.
confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.practicalTakeaway: Do not treat room mics as optional ambience; design the session around them.

confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.confirmedInformation: An extreme/furthest room mic could be mono and heavily compressed.
confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.practicalTakeaway: Set up one mono far-room mic specifically for compression and blend.

confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.confirmedInformation: Close mics were used, but exact models and placements are not public.
confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.practicalTakeaway: Use conventional close mics as the punch foundation.

confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.confirmedInformation: 24-track 2-inch 456 tape was used before bounces/transfers into Pro Tools.
confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.practicalTakeaway: Tape or tape-like saturation belongs in the practical target.

confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.confirmedInformation: Mixing came from Pro Tools to the Neotek console and back into Pro Tools.
confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.practicalTakeaway: Analog-console or console-style stem routing is appropriate.

confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.confirmedInformation: Tarquin had 1176, LA4, Tube-Tech CL1B, Neve 33609, Distressors, and dbx 162; Katis described using the dbx 162 heavily for drums.
confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.confidence: confirmed studio context / medium Antics specificity
confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.practicalTakeaway: Have fast FET, VCA/dbx-style, and parallel room compression options.

confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.confirmedInformation: Used a little, not to replace sounds they worked hard to capture.
confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.practicalTakeaway: Samples are allowed only as subtle support.

confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.confirmedInformation: Click use varied by song or section; some tracks used no click.
confirmedMicAndWorkflowFacts.practicalTakeaway: Preserve human tempo movement where useful.

recommendedModernReproduction.pattern: cardioid or hypercardioid depending on mic
recommendedModernReproduction.placement: Inside the drum or just at the port, aimed toward the beater but not overly focused on click.
recommendedModernReproduction.phaseRelationship: Check polarity and timing against kick out, overheads, and room mics.
recommendedModernReproduction.processingNotes: Moderate EQ and compression. Avoid exaggerated modern metal click.

recommendedModernReproduction.pattern: cardioid, omni, or figure-8 depending on mic and room
recommendedModernReproduction.phaseRelationship: Check polarity against kick in; do not assume polarity flip is automatically correct.
recommendedModernReproduction.processingNotes: Blend for low-end weight and air.

recommendedModernReproduction.placement: 1-3 inches above rim, angled across the head.
recommendedModernReproduction.phaseRelationship: Check against overheads and snare bottom.
recommendedModernReproduction.processingNotes: Medium compression only if needed. Preserve ghost notes and dynamics.

recommendedModernReproduction.phaseRelationship: Usually polarity-reversed against snare top, but confirm by ear.

recommendedModernReproduction.placement: Close, above rim, angled toward strike zone.
recommendedModernReproduction.phaseRelationship: Check against overheads and room mics.
recommendedModernReproduction.processingNotes: Gate lightly only if arrangement requires it. Avoid disconnecting toms from the room.

recommendedModernReproduction.pattern: cardioid, omni, or figure-8 depending on room
recommendedModernReproduction.placement: Start with spaced pair or ORTF. Keep cymbals controlled.
recommendedModernReproduction.phaseRelationship: Measure or check snare-centered arrival if using spaced pair.
recommendedModernReproduction.processingNotes: Do not over-brighten. Cymbals should sit inside the band rather than above it.

recommendedModernReproduction.phaseRelationship: Check mono compatibility and relationship to close mics.
recommendedModernReproduction.processingNotes: Mild compression. Blend for width and size.

recommendedModernReproduction.pattern: session pattern unknown; choose figure-8, omni, or cardioid by room
recommendedModernReproduction.placement: Farthest useful room position, hallway, doorway, stairwell, or reflective adjoining space.
recommendedModernReproduction.phaseRelationship: Do not automatically time-align. Listen to natural delay as part of groove and size.
recommendedModernReproduction.processingNotes: Compress aggressively with 1176, Distressor, dbx 160/162-style compression, or equivalent. Blend under close mics.

recommendedModernReproduction.placement: Reflective corner, hallway, floor, stairwell, or behind a door.
recommendedModernReproduction.phaseRelationship: Check against room and close mics.
recommendedModernReproduction.processingNotes: Crush, distort, filter, or gate. Optional.

confirmed: 24-track 2-inch 456 tape before Pro Tools transfer/bounce
mixing from Pro Tools out to Neotek console and back into Pro Tools
final mastering by Greg Calbi at Sterling Sound
confirmedTarquinContext: 1176, LA4, Tube-Tech CL1B, Neve 33609, Distressor, dbx 162 outboard context
signalChain.practicalSubstitute: Track through a console or analog-style preamps with musical headroom. Use tape if available, otherwise use conservative tape saturation. Keep the chain warm and dimensional, not pristine and sterile.

variations.name: Brass snare variation
variations.notes: Use a 6.5 inch brass snare or equivalent deeper metal snare for bigger, heavier, more authoritative songs.
variations.confidence: confirmed source snare / inferred song use

variations.name: Wood snare variation
variations.notes: Use a 5 inch wood snare or equivalent tighter/drier snare for more contained songs.
variations.confidence: confirmed source snare / inferred song use

variations.name: Altec-style extreme room
variations.notes: Darker, characterful mono far-room color.

variations.name: ELA M 251-style extreme room
variations.notes: Smoother, more hi-fi tube-condenser far-room color.

variations.name: Selective click
variations.notes: Use click only where arrangement precision needs it; preserve rushing/slowing where the band feels better.

variations.name: Sparse reinforcement
variations.notes: Use Sound Replacer-style support sparingly and never as the main drum identity.
variations.confidence: confirmed general use / unknown song details

songBySongInferences.inference: Controlled and spacious; room blend should stay subtle and intimate.
Tight kick/snare articulation is essential; room adds motion without blurring groove.
Dry, forward snare and controlled cymbals; room adds body, not explosion.
More atmosphere can be allowed as sections expand.
Prioritize fast transient clarity and tight low-end control; tuck compressed room underneath.
Deeper brass snare and more crushed mono room are practical.
Use room mics for space but avoid over-compression that pumps unnaturally.
Punchy but not oversized; snare should leave room for guitars and vocal.
Rhythmic clarity matters more than huge ambience; keep rooms filtered/compressed to avoid clouding motion.

songBySongInferences.song: A Time to Be So Small
songBySongInferences.inference: Distant/atmospheric room character may help, but drum image should remain understated.

albumRationale: The reproducible lesson is recording architecture, not exact undocumented close mics.
Use a real drummer with prepared parts and a familiar kit.
Leave the room open around the drums.
Use close mics for definition and serious room mics for breath and size.
Capture a mono crushed far-room option.
Use sample reinforcement only as subtle support.
Use tape, tape-like saturation, or analog-console-style routing without flattening the performance.
studioBookingQuestions: Do you have a medium live room with wood floor or lively surfaces?
Can guitar and bass amps be isolated while the drummer remains visually connected to the band?
Is there a hallway, stairwell, doorway, or distant room position for a mono far-room mic?
Do you have ribbons or tube condensers suitable for room mics?
Do you have compressors suitable for crushed room sounds, such as 1176, Distressor, dbx 160/162, or equivalents?
Can we track to tape, or create a tape-like saturation stage during tracking or mix?
Can the engineer print or monitor compressed room mics during tracking?
Can we avoid over-editing and preserve human tempo movement where appropriate?
Can we set up two snare options and switch by song?
Can we audition room mics before committing to drum sounds?
unresolvedQuestions: Exact hi-hat or ride spot mics.
Exact room mic distances and heights.
Exact compressor used on the far-room mic.
Exact tape speed and noise-reduction details.
Exact drum kit brand/model, kick size, tom sizes, cymbals, sticks, and muffling.

name: Yeah Yeah Yeahs - Fever to Tell / Headgear raw-trio drum configuration

candidateSetupLabels.name: Fever to Tell Minimum Raw Trio
candidateSetupLabels.note: Label and channel grouping only; no planner coordinates created in this pass.

candidateSetupLabels.name: Fever to Tell Expanded Headgear

accuracyBoundary.summary: Main confirmed facts come from album-credit-derived sources and studio-history sources. Exact drum microphones, kit, cymbals, mic placements, preamps, tape machine, drum processing, and room setup were not publicly documented in reliable sources checked.
confirmedScope: review-language support for guitar/drums rock and forceful percussion
undocumentedScope: exact preamps, console, compression, tape/digital format
samples, triggers, quantization, or drum replacement

sources.title: Fever to Tell
sources.reliability: medium; useful for album-credit-derived personnel, studio, producer, engineer, mixing, mastering, and track-editing context
supports: David Andrew Sitek and Yeah Yeah Yeahs production
Alan Moulder and Nick Zinner mixing tracks 1-7, 9, 10
David Andrew Sitek mixing tracks 8 and 11

sources.title: Headgear Studio

supports: Fever to Tell recorded at Headgear with David Sitek
Headgear founded in 1998 and closed in 2012

sources.title: Yeah Yeah Yeahs
sources.reliability: medium; useful for band lineup and broad context, not drum-session documentation
supports: Karen O / Nick Zinner / Brian Chase trio context
early Yeah Yeah Yeahs band history

sources.title: Yeah Yeah Yeahs: Fever to Tell Album Review
sources.reliability: medium for descriptive sound language; not technical session documentation

sources.title: Karen O Has Found a More Joyful Kind of Wildness

Maps demo was recorded into four-track
demo-origin context is separate from final album drum mic documentation

artistAlbumEngineer.producer: David Andrew Sitek and Yeah Yeah Yeahs
artistAlbumEngineer.mixStudio: Eden Studios, London, for Alan Moulder/Nick Zinner mixes
artistAlbumEngineer.mixing: Alan Moulder and Nick Zinner on tracks 1-7, 9, and 10; David Andrew Sitek on tracks 8 and 11
artistAlbumEngineer.mastering: Howie Weinberg at Masterdisk, New York
artistAlbumEngineer.editing: Roger Lian credited for track/digital editing; exact drum-editing role not publicly documented

soundGoal.summary: Raw, fast, midrange-forward, physical drums that sound like a band playing in a room rather than a polished modern rock production.
qualities: room/crush mics add danger or explosion only where useful
soundGoal.confidence: inferred from user research and review descriptions

confirmed: Fever to Tell was recorded at Headgear Studio in Williamsburg/Brooklyn, New York.
roomContext.practicalSubstituteFromNotes: Small-to-medium live room with attitude, fast reflections, and controlled decay. Wood, brick, plaster, or concrete room with movable absorption. Avoid a huge polished rock room unless room mics can stay tight and aggressive.

presets.label: Headgear Brooklyn tracking-room approximation
presets.source: Tape Op documents Headgear as a subdivided Williamsburg building with 2,000 sq ft studio zones and Fever to Tell as an early session; exact live-room dimensions were not found.

presets.label: Headgear Brooklyn tracking-room approximation
presets.source: Same Headgear room approximation, with added hallway/far-room positions for the expanded app layout. Gobo use is not confirmed.

confirmed: Brian Chase played drums.
practicalSubstituteFromNotes: one drier/fatter snare for restrained songs such as Maps
controlled and musical cymbals rather than bright or washy cymbals

micList.placement: Inside the port if resonant head is ported, or just outside resonant head if unported. Aim off-center toward beater impact.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Around beater height; 2-8 inches from resonant head or just inside shell; angle slightly away from excessive click if drummer plays hard.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check polarity against mono overhead or main kit mic first, then against room mics. Kick should gain punch when combined.
micList.processingNotes: Avoid overly modern kick. Use modest 60-80 Hz shaping only if needed, remove sub-rumble, and add beater definition only enough for tempo clarity. Avoid sample replacement unless intentionally departing from original approach.
micList.evidenceBasis: No reliable public source found confirms original kick mic or placement.

micList.placement: Above rim, pointed toward center, with null aimed toward hi-hat.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 1-2 inches above head, 30-45 degrees downward.
micList.phaseRelationship: Align by ear with overhead or mono kit mic. If mono overhead is main kit picture, tune snare polarity to that mic rather than only close kick.
micList.processingNotes: High-pass only as needed. Preserve midrange crack. Fast but non-flattening compression; a few dB from 1176-style or dbx-style compressor if printing.
micList.evidenceBasis: No reliable public source found confirms original snare mic or placement.

micList.placement: Under snare, aimed at wires.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 2-4 inches below bottom head, angled to reduce kick spill.
micList.phaseRelationship: Polarity usually inverted relative to snare top; verify by ear.
micList.processingNotes: Blend only for wire articulation. Do not make snare too slick or modern.

micList.placement: Close, above rim, angled toward center.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 1-3 inches above head, 30-60 degrees downward.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against overhead or mono kit mic. Toms should add body without hollowing kit image.
micList.processingNotes: Avoid aggressive gating while tracking. Use editing or automation later if necessary.
micList.evidenceBasis: No reliable public source found confirms original tom mics, tom sizes, or whether close tom mics were used.

micList.pattern: figure-8 for ribbons; cardioid for condensers/dynamics depending on room
micList.placement: Above kit, centered around snare/kick relationship rather than cymbal glamour.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 3-5 ft above snare, aimed between snare and kick, adjusted until kick and snare feel balanced.
micList.phaseRelationship: Make this the phase anchor. Add close mics around it. Check that kick and snare do not hollow out when close mics are introduced.
micList.processingNotes: Compress lightly to moderately if it creates forward motion. Roll off harsh top end if cymbals dominate.
micList.evidenceBasis: No source confirms this exact technique; research notes identify it as a practical compact/narrow kit anchor.

micList.pattern: cardioid, figure-8, or omni depending on room
micList.placement: Narrow spaced pair, ORTF, or low/wide pair kept controlled. Avoid exaggerated modern width.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 3-5 ft above cymbals; start lower than hi-fi rock setup and adjust to reduce splash.
micList.phaseRelationship: Keep both overheads equidistant from snare if using spaced pair. Check mono compatibility.
micList.processingNotes: High-pass enough to leave kick/snare close mics stable, but do not remove all body.
micList.evidenceBasis: Original overhead method is not publicly documented.

micList.pattern: cardioid, figure-8, or omni depending on room
micList.placement: In front of kit, aimed between kick and snare.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 3-8 ft in front of kit, 3-5 ft high. Start near drummer chest height facing kit.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check polarity and timing against kick, snare, and overhead. Use polarity with more low-mid punch. Delay-align only if it improves impact.
micList.processingNotes: Use as parallel aggression channel. Try 1176 all-buttons, Distressor, dbx 160, LA-3A, TG-style limiting, tape saturation, or console distortion. Blend under close mics.
micList.evidenceBasis: No source confirms a crush mic or room mic; listed as practical substitute in research notes.

micList.placement: Far end of live room, hallway, stairwell, or reflective boundary if available.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 10-25 ft from kit if room allows; lower placement for thump, higher placement for cymbal/air.
micList.phaseRelationship: Treat as effect return. Do not force phase-perfect if lateness creates size.
micList.processingNotes: Heavy compression, saturation, or gating for choruses/noise sections. Mute or lower for tight verses.
micList.evidenceBasis: No reliable public source found documents far-room or hallway mics on album.

micList.placement: Above outer edge of hats, aimed away from snare.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 4-8 inches above hats, angled across rather than straight down.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against snare top and overhead.
micList.processingNotes: High-pass aggressively. Use only if arrangement needs extra hat articulation; otherwise leave muted.
micList.evidenceBasis: No reliable public source found confirms a hi-hat mic.

variations.name: Minimum viable setup
variations.notes: Kick, snare top, mono overhead, mono front-of-kit/crush mic.

variations.name: Expanded setup
variations.notes: Kick, snare top/bottom, rack tom, floor tom, mono overhead, narrow stereo overheads, front-of-kit crush mic, one room/hall mic, optional hi-hat.

variations.name: Maps variation
variations.notes: Less crush, less room, softer cymbal balance, restrained snare/kick image. Karen O described a drum-machine/four-track demo origin; this does not document final-album drum-machine replacement.
variations.confidence: demo origin confirmed / drum production inference

variations.name: Y Control variation
variations.notes: Room/crush compression can be pushed harder. Pitchfork review language supports forceful percussion as a descriptive reference, not session technique documentation.

variations.name: Fast songs variation
variations.notes: Date with the Night, Rich, Tick, Black Tongue, and Pin prioritize transient urgency, controlled cymbal wash, and midrange snare/kick.

variations.name: No No No variation
variations.notes: Automate room/crush mic upward during chaotic sections and keep close kit more controlled in tighter sections.

unknowns: Snare model, size, material, tuning, and dampening.
Drumheads, sticks, tape, wallets, gels, blankets, rings, tea towels, or other muffling.
Console, preamps, compressors, EQ, tape machine, tape speed, tape formulation, converters.
Samples, triggers, quantization, or drum replacement.
finalPracticalChecklist: Use a simple, well-tuned rock kit.
Use one rack tom and one floor tom unless the arrangement demands more.
Bring two snares: one cutting/ringy and one drier/fatter.
Use controlled cymbals, not overly bright or washy cymbals.
Book a small-to-medium live room with fast reflections and character.
Start with kick, snare top, mono overhead, and mono front-of-kit/crush mic.
Add stereo overheads, tom mics, snare bottom, far room, and hi-hat only as needed.
Make mono overhead or front-of-kit mic the emotional center of the drum sound.
Check phase from the mono kit picture outward.
Use compression for urgency, not polish.
Good compression options: 1176-style, dbx-style, Distressor-style, LA-3A-style, TG-style, console/tape saturation.
Automate or blend room/crush mics differently by song section.

name: Gang of Four / Solid Gold - dry-close, dense funk-punk drum configuration

soundGoal.summary: Tight, dry-to-semi-dry, dense, controlled, rhythmically exact funk-punk drums. The kit supports bass/guitar interlock rather than a large open rock drum picture.
sourceContext: Jon King described the target as heavy full-on funk with loud guitar.
Hugo Burnham described What We All Want as the band working out its funk with Jimmy Douglass.
soundGoal.confidence: inferred from source context and research notes

confirmed: Solid Gold was recorded at Abbey Road, London.
unknown: exact Abbey Road room used for drums
whether Studio Two, Studio Three, or another Abbey Road space was used
roomContext.practicalSubstituteFromNotes: Controlled medium live room, not a huge reflective rock room and not a fully dead booth. Some natural air, controlled with gobos, rugs, and baffles.

presets.label: Abbey Road Studio Two context
presets.confidence: confirmed Studio Two dimensions / exact Solid Gold room unknown
presets.source: Tape Op cites Studio Two at approximately 38 ft x 60 ft with a 24 ft ceiling and 1.2 s reverb; Solid Gold sources credit Abbey Road but do not confirm the exact room.

presets.label: Abbey Road Studio Two context
presets.confidence: confirmed Studio Two dimensions / exact Solid Gold room unknown
presets.source: Same Abbey Road Studio Two dimensional context for the expanded app preset. Gobo use is not confirmed for the original Solid Gold drum room.

confirmed: Hugo Burnham played drums.
practicalSubstituteFromNotes: 14 inch snare tuned medium-high with short decay
avoid huge open cymbals, long tom sustain, and large room bloom

micList.placement: If kick is ported, just inside the hole. If unported, 4-8 inches outside resonant head. Aim between beater impact and shell center.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Around beater height. Angle slightly off-axis if attack is too sharp.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against overhead, snare, and any front-of-kit mic. Flip polarity if low end disappears when combined.
micList.processingNotes: Moderate internal damping. Avoid long boom. Add 60-80 Hz only if needed. Cut 250-450 Hz boxiness. Add 2-4 kHz only enough for definition. Avoid modern sub-heavy kick.

micList.placement: 1-2 inches above rim, angled 30-45 degrees toward center. Aim rear null toward hi-hat.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Close and low for dry crack; slightly higher for more shell tone.
micList.phaseRelationship: Use snare top as kit phase anchor. Check against overhead and snare bottom if used.
micList.processingNotes: Tune medium-high with short decay. Use small damping if ring interferes with guitar/bass rhythm. Compress lightly to moderately. Avoid huge gated 1980s snare sound.

micList.placement: 1-2 inches below wires, angled away from kick and hi-hat bleed.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Close to wires, not aimed at shell.
micList.phaseRelationship: Invert polarity relative to snare top, then check by ear.
micList.processingNotes: Blend quietly for wire articulation. High-pass aggressively. Mute if it makes kit too bright or modern.

micList.placement: 4-8 inches above outer edge of hats, aimed slightly downward and away from snare.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Close but not hyped. Avoid pointing directly at bell unless sharp tick is needed.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check with snare top and overhead.
micList.processingNotes: Use less hi-hat mic than expected. Rhythmic clarity should come from playing and arrangement. High-pass heavily. Use little or no compression.

micList.placement: 1-2 inches above rim, aimed between center and edge.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 30-45 degrees downward.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against overhead and floor tom.
micList.processingNotes: Short controlled sustain. Avoid stadium toms. Gate only if needed; manual cleanup is better than obvious gating.

micList.placement: 1-3 inches above rim, angled toward center.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Close enough to reduce room wash.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against overhead and kick.
micList.processingNotes: Tune for short low-mid punctuation, not long resonance. Cut mud if it masks bass.

micList.pattern: figure-8 for ribbon, cardioid for dynamic or condenser
micList.placement: Mono overhead or narrow stereo. For mono, 3-4 ft above snare, centered so snare and kick feel coherent. Aim at kick/snare relationship rather than cymbals. For stereo, use XY or narrow pair rather than wide spaced overheads.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Lower than roomy rock setup. High enough to capture kit image, low enough to keep room controlled.
micList.phaseRelationship: Make this image mic and align close mics around it. Measure from snare to overhead and check in mono.
micList.processingNotes: Keep cymbals dark and controlled. Roll off excessive top end if overhead sounds too modern. Use little compression unless it improves groove.

micList.pattern: figure-8 for ribbon, cardioid or omni depending on mic and room
micList.placement: 3-6 ft in front of kit, around kick-to-chest height, aimed between kick and snare.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Below cymbal plane to avoid excessive cymbal wash.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check polarity against kick, snare, and overhead. Delay/slide only if it improves punch without modernizing the image.
micList.processingNotes: Compress hard only if blended quietly. This channel should thicken groove, not turn kit into big room-rock sound.

micList.source: Far room mic, optional and usually low in mix
micList.pattern: any pattern that minimizes cymbal harshness
micList.placement: 8-15 ft from kit only if room sounds controlled and flattering.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Chest height or slightly above. Avoid high cymbal-heavy placement.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check in mono. Delay is optional but should not create a polished modern image.
micList.processingNotes: Blend very low, around 0-10 percent. Mute if drum sound loses dry, mechanical, interlocking quality.

overallProcessingNotes: Treat as analog, close-controlled, pre-sample production.
No reliable evidence was found for samples, triggers, drum replacement, quantization, or digital editing on Solid Gold.
Use tape or tape-style saturation, console EQ, and restrained compression.
Modern equivalents from research notes include Neve/API/Trident/TG-style preamps or console paths, 1176/DBX/RS124/Fairchild-style compression, and dark short plate or chamber reverb only if needed.
Avoid huge sub kick, ultra-bright cymbals, hard sample-like snare, wide stereo room mics, and obvious gated reverb.

variations.name: What We All Want
variations.notes: Main reference: dense, funk-driven, kick/snare/bass-forward, controlled cymbal energy.

variations.name: Paralysed
variations.notes: Use less room and overhead, keep snare dry, and leave arrangement space exposed.

variations.name: Outside the Trains Don't Run on Time / He'd Send in the Army
variations.notes: Public sources identify these as re-recordings of earlier singles; no reliable song-specific mic or mix documentation found.
variations.confidence: confirmed re-recording context / unknown technical setup

studioBookingChecklist: Ask for a controlled medium live room.
Ask for gobos, rugs, and baffles.
Use a compact drum kit.
Use a controlled 14 inch snare with short decay.
Use tight hi-hats and restrained cymbals.
Prioritize dynamic and ribbon mics over very bright condensers.
Use close mics on kick, snare, toms, and hi-hat.
Use mono or narrow stereo overheads.
Add a front-of-kit mic only if it helps density.
Use room mics sparingly or not at all.
Use analog-style preamps or console color.
Use light to moderate compression.
Use short dark reverb only if needed.
Check phase constantly in mono.
Do not rely on sample replacement.
Do not over-edit the performance.
Keep drums rhythmically exposed.
unresolvedQuestions: Exact Abbey Road room used for drums.
Exact kit brand and drum sizes.
Exact snare, cymbals, heads, sticks, and muffling.
Exact console path, preamps, EQ, compression, tape speed, and tape formulation.

soundGoal.summary: Tight, focused, punchy, controlled, dry, and somewhat claustrophobic rock drums built from real performances with separately recorded shells and cymbals.
confirmedMethod: Dave Grohl played acoustic drum shells while hitting electronic cymbal pads instead of real cymbals.
Real cymbals were recorded separately while dummy snare and padded toms minimized shell spill.
Eric Valentine stated that there were no drum samples.
practicalTarget: real cymbals layered as a separate performance pass
human but highly controllable rock drums

confirmed: The documented drum sound is associated with a notably dead, controlled drum capture rather than a large ambient room.
Album credits list The Site, Conway, and Barefoot as studios.
unknown: gobos, baffles, risers, absorbers, or blankets
which credited studio/room produced each drum track
roomContext.practicalSubstitute: Use a small-to-medium dead room, iso room, or heavily controlled live room. Avoid a splashy high-ceiling room unless it can be made much drier with gobos, rugs, absorbers, and blankets.
roomContext.confidence: confirmed sonic direction / exact room unknown

presets.label: Controlled dead iso-room approximation
presets.source: Eric Valentine described the No One Knows drum sound as notably dead with shells and cymbals recorded separately; no exact room dimensions were found.

presets.label: Controlled dead iso-room approximation
presets.source: Same dry isolation-room substitute with additional close, cymbal-spot, and dead-room planning channels.

confirmed: Dave Grohl played drums on most of the album.
practicalSubstitute: 22 or 24 inch kick with controlled internal damping
medium-low rock snare with short ring plus a brighter crack snare option
rack/floor toms tuned for punch rather than long resonance
cymbals chosen for tone because they are overdubbed separately

micOptions: Electronic cymbal pads, not final audio mics
micList.placement: Pads placed where cymbals would normally be so the shell performance preserves normal reach and motion.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Normal cymbal-playing height and reach.
micList.phaseRelationship: No final audio phase issue if pads are not used in the final mix.
micList.processingNotes: Confirmed central method: Grohl played acoustic shells while hitting electronic cymbal pads instead of real cymbals.

micList.placement: Inside the kick or at the port.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 2-12 inches from beater impact area; angle to balance click and body.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check polarity against kick out, shell overhead/front mic, and bass later.
micList.processingNotes: Close, punchy, controlled. Compression and gating can be more assertive because cymbal bleed is reduced.

micList.pattern: cardioid, omni, or figure-8 depending on room
micList.placement: Outside resonant head.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 6-24 inches from head; farther only if the room is controlled.
micList.phaseRelationship: Polarity/time-align against kick in; avoid hollow low end.
micList.processingNotes: Optional. Use only if it adds weight without boxiness.

micList.placement: Above batter head near rim.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 1-3 inches above head, angled 30-60 degrees toward center; aim null toward hat/pad zone.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against snare bottom and any overhead/front mic.
micList.processingNotes: Core sound source. EQ and compression can be more aggressive than in a normal cymbal-bleed-heavy kit take.

micList.placement: Under snare, aimed toward wires.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 1-3 inches below bottom head.
micList.phaseRelationship: Usually polarity-invert relative to top, then fine-adjust by ear.
micList.processingNotes: Optional rattle/brightness layer. Keep controlled.

micList.placement: Close over batter heads.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 1-3 inches above rim, angled toward strike zone.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against shell overhead/front mic.
micList.processingNotes: Gate, expand, compress, and EQ as needed because cymbal bleed is reduced. Tune and damp before EQ.

micList.placement: Close over batter heads.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 1-4 inches above rim, angled for attack/body balance.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against kick and any mono kit mic.
micList.processingNotes: Manage low mids carefully; avoid long uncontrolled decay.

micList.source: Shell image / front-of-kit / mono kit mic
micList.pattern: cardioid, figure-8, or omni depending on room
micList.placement: In front of kit or above kick/snare axis.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 2-6 ft from kit, chest to head height; move until kick/snare balance works.
micList.phaseRelationship: Use as phase anchor or mute if it clouds the close mics.
micList.processingNotes: Optional glue/crush mic. Useful as a practical substitute, but not publicly documented for the album.

micList.pattern: cardioid, omni, or figure-8 depending on room and cymbal tone
micList.placement: Over real cymbals during the second pass.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Start 2-4 ft above cymbals. Use spaced pair for width or ORTF/XY for tighter mono image.
micList.phaseRelationship: Phase is mainly within the cymbal pair. Align musically to the shell pass, but avoid over-editing feel.
micList.processingNotes: Confirmed concept: real cymbals were recorded separately with dummy snare and padded toms, then blended with the shell pass. Exact overhead technique is unknown.

micList.placement: Close to specific cymbals during cymbal pass.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 4-12 inches from cymbal edge or bell area; avoid harsh edge blast.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check against cymbal overheads.
micList.processingNotes: Optional. Since cymbals are isolated, spot mics can be blended low or omitted.

micList.placement: In the dead room, hallway, or adjacent space only if useful.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: 6-15 ft from kit for a small room; farther only if there is a usable hallway or chamber.
micList.phaseRelationship: Check polarity against close shells; time delay may be part of character.
micList.processingNotes: Use as parallel dirt, not the foundation.

micList.source: Dummy snare / padded toms during cymbal pass
micOptions: No final drum-shell mic source; this is a physical overdub aid
micList.placement: Dummy snare and padded toms replace normal shell sound during cymbal take.
micList.heightDistanceAngle: Normal kit geometry.
micList.phaseRelationship: Timing relationship is performance-based, not mic phase-based.
micList.processingNotes: Confirmed method: real cymbals were recorded while dummy/padded drums minimized shell spill.

phaseRelationship: No public source found gives a detailed phase strategy, mic distance rule, overhead geometry, or polarity chart.
Treat the shell pass as the primary drum image.
Check kick in/out, snare top/bottom, toms, and any mono kit mic first.
Add the cymbal pass and check whether the groove feels late, early, or smeared.
Do not automatically sample-align every cymbal transient to the shell pass; slight human variance may be part of the feel.
overallProcessingNotes: Confirmed: Eric Valentine stated that the drums were not sample-replaced.

Confirmed: Valentine described a frequency-arrangement concept where guitars occupied a concentrated midrange area, with Homme focused around roughly 600-700 Hz, pushing drums into a more scooped and controlled role.
Unknown: exact preamps, console channels, EQs, compressors, gates, tape machine, tape speed, converters, drum bus compression, reverb, and plug-ins.
Practical translation: use assertive close-mic EQ and compression, keep shells dry and physical, avoid modern-metal click, and blend cymbals as a separate layer.

variations.notes: Best-documented configuration: dry shell pass plus separate real-cymbal pass.

whatMattersMost: willingness to record shells and cymbals separately
small dead room or controlled iso space
frequency arrangement around dense midrange guitars

Confirm the drummer can repeat shell and cymbal passes consistently.
Prepare electronic cymbal pads for the shell pass.
Prepare dummy snare and padded toms for the cymbal pass.
Use a loud rock kit with short sustain.
Prioritize close kick, snare, and tom capture.
Record cymbal overheads separately.
Use room mics only as dirt or optional depth.
Avoid default sample replacement.
unresolvedQuestions: Exact kit, snare, cymbals, heads, sticks, muffling, and tuning.
Exact console path, preamps, EQ, compression, gates, tape speed, tape formulation, converters, and plug-ins.
